questions.stem: What are the four fundamental Python data types?
options: String, Array, Object, Function
int (integer), float (decimal), str (text), bool (True/False)
number, text, boolean, none
char, double, varchar, boolean
questions.explanation: Python's four core scalar types: int (whole numbers: 1, -42, 1_000_000), float (decimals: 3.14, 1e-4), str (text: 'hello', f-strings), bool (True/False — subclass of int). Everything in Python is an object of some type; type(x) returns the type. ML adds many more: torch.Tensor, np.ndarray, pd.DataFrame — all built on these primitives.

questions.stem: What is the difference between a Python list and a NumPy array?
options: Lists can hold multiple items; arrays can only hold one

Lists hold arbitrary Python objects with no type constraint; NumPy arrays hold a single numeric dtype (float32, int8, etc.) in a contiguous memory block — enabling SIMD vectorisation and BLAS acceleration
NumPy arrays can only hold integers; lists can hold any type
They are identical — NumPy array is just a faster name for list
questions.explanation: Python list: [1, 'hello', 3.14] — heterogeneous, elements are Python objects with type overhead, non-contiguous. NumPy array: dtype=float32, elements packed in C-contiguous memory, no Python overhead per element. For 1M elements: list = ~56 MB (8 bytes pointer + object overhead); np.float32 array = 4 MB. NumPy can call BLAS/SIMD because the data is homogeneous and contiguous.

questions.stem: Why do PyTorch models use torch.long (int64) for token IDs but torch.float32 (or float16) for weights?
options: PyTorch randomly assigns types and you cannot change them
Token IDs are indices (integers, up to vocab_size ~50000); weights are continuous values requiring decimal precision — using float for indices wastes memory and using int for weights loses precision
GPU only supports int64 for all operations
It is a performance optimisation with no correctness impact
questions.explanation: Token IDs are whole numbers (indices into a vocabulary of ~50,000 tokens) — int64 is appropriate. Using float32 for indices would waste memory and cause precision issues for large vocabularies. Model weights represent continuous learned values — float32/float16/bfloat16 provide the necessary decimal precision. Embedding layers specifically require torch.long input: nn.Embedding(vocab_size, d_model) will error on float inputs.

questions.stem: What does .to(device) do when training a model in PyTorch?
options: Converts the tensor's data type (e.g. float32 to float16)
Moves the tensor or model's data from one device to another (CPU ↔ GPU) — all tensors in an operation must be on the same device
Saves the tensor to a file on disk
Creates a copy of the tensor without modifying the original
questions.explanation: .to('cuda') or .to('cpu') moves the underlying data between CPU RAM and GPU VRAM. If you call model(x) with model on GPU and x on CPU, PyTorch raises a RuntimeError. The pattern: device = 'cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu'; model.to(device); x = x.to(device). .to() can also change dtype: tensor.to(torch.float16) — useful for mixed precision.

questions.stem: What happens when you store image pixels as float32 instead of uint8 in a dataset?
options: Nothing — float32 and uint8 produce identical results for images
4× more memory is used: uint8 = 1 byte/pixel, float32 = 4 bytes/pixel — a 224×224 RGB image uses 150 KB as uint8 but 600 KB as float32
The image cannot be displayed because monitors only understand uint8
Training will be slower because float32 is not supported on GPU
questions.explanation: uint8 pixels (0-255) use 1 byte each. float32 uses 4 bytes. A 224×224×3 image: uint8 = 150 KB, float32 = 601 KB. For a dataset of 1M images: uint8 = 150 GB, float32 = 600 GB. Best practice: store images as uint8, load as uint8 in DataLoader, cast to float32 and normalise (divide by 255) only right before the model. This saves 4× RAM and DataLoader I/O bandwidth.

questions.stem: What is bfloat16 and why is it preferred over float16 for training large models?
options: bfloat16 is more widely supported by older GPUs than float16

bfloat16 keeps float32's 8-bit exponent (same numeric range ±3.4×10³⁸) while reducing mantissa to 7 bits — preventing gradient overflow that float16 (5-bit exponent, max ±65504) frequently suffers during training
bfloat16 is actually 16-bit integer format, not floating point
bfloat16 uses less VRAM than float16
questions.explanation: float16 limits: exponent 5 bits → max value 65504. Gradients and activations in large models regularly exceed this, producing inf. bfloat16 copies float32's 8-bit exponent → max value 3.4×10³⁸ — same as float32, so overflow never occurs in practice. Fewer mantissa bits (7 vs 10) means less precision, but training with bf16 + AMP is now standard for LLMs. float16 requires loss scaling to compensate for overflow risk.
